subject: ap-chemistry
theme: The arrangement of electrons in bonds and around atoms determines molecular shape, polarity, and the physical properties that arise from intermolecular forces.

core_concepts.title: Lewis Diagrams and Formal Charge
core_concepts.detail: A Lewis diagram places valence electrons as lone pairs and bonding pairs. Formal charge = (valence electrons) − (lone pair electrons) − ½(bonding electrons). Structures with formal charges closest to zero are preferred.

core_concepts.title: Resonance and Bond Order
core_concepts.detail: When a single Lewis structure does not adequately represent a molecule, resonance structures are drawn. The real molecule is a hybrid with bond orders between 1 and 2 (or other integers). Resonance structures always have the same atom connectivity — only electron placement differs.

core_concepts.title: VSEPR Theory and Molecular Geometry
core_concepts.detail: VSEPR: electron pairs (bonding and lone) repel each other and adopt geometries that minimize repulsion. Lone pair–bonding pair repulsion > bonding pair–bonding pair repulsion, compressing bond angles. Common geometries: linear (2), trigonal planar (3), tetrahedral (4), trigonal bipyramidal (5), octahedral (6).

core_concepts.title: Hybridization
core_concepts.detail: Hybridization describes the mixing of atomic orbitals to form new hybrid orbitals. $sp$ (linear), $sp^2$ (trigonal planar), $sp^3$ (tetrahedral), $sp^3d$ (trigonal bipyramidal), $sp^3d^2$ (octahedral). The number of hybrid orbitals equals the number of electron groups around the central atom.

core_concepts.title: Sigma and Pi Bonds
core_concepts.detail: A $\sigma$ bond forms by end-to-end overlap along the internuclear axis; it allows free rotation. A $\pi$ bond forms by side-to-side overlap of parallel p orbitals; it restricts rotation. Single bond = 1 $\sigma$; double bond = 1 $\sigma$ + 1 $\pi$; triple bond = 1 $\sigma$ + 2 $\pi$.

core_concepts.title: Polarity of Bonds and Molecules
core_concepts.detail: Bond polarity arises from electronegativity differences between bonded atoms. A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds AND the bond dipoles do not cancel due to asymmetric geometry. Symmetric molecules (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CCl}_4$) are nonpolar despite having polar bonds.

core_concepts.title: Solid Types
core_concepts.detail: Ionic solids: charged ions in a lattice, high melting point, conduct when molten. Covalent network solids: extended 3D covalent bonds (diamond, $\text{SiO}_2$), very high melting points, non-conductors. Molecular solids: held by weak IMFs, low melting points. Metallic solids: metal cations in sea of electrons, conduct in all states.

formulas.name: Formal charge
formulas.description: Formal charge equals valence electrons ($V$) minus lone pair electrons ($L$) minus half the bonding electrons ($B$) on that atom.
formulas.example: Oxygen in water has $V = 6$, $L = 4$ (2 lone pairs), $B = 4$ (2 bonds × 2). $FC = 6 - 4 - 2 = 0$.

formulas.name: Bond order
formulas.description: For a single bond between two atoms in a Lewis structure. In resonance hybrids, bond order is the average across all resonance structures.
formulas.example: In $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$, 3 resonance structures share one double bond: average bond order = $(1 + 1 + 2)/3 = 4/3 \approx 1.33$.

exam_tip: VSEPR geometry questions often hinge on lone pairs. The electron geometry (all pairs) determines hybridization; the molecular geometry (bonding pairs only) determines shape. For example, water has tetrahedral electron geometry ($sp^3$) but bent molecular geometry. Always state both when asked.
